chunk_summary: Guidelines for endocrine and genetic testing in pediatric obesity emphasize phenotype-driven evaluation, avoiding routine panels, and addressing common interpretation errors to prevent misdiagnosis and treatment delays.
learning_objectives: Understand when endocrine testing is indicated in pediatric obesity based on clinical features.
Identify common pitfalls in interpreting thyroid function tests in obese children.
Recognize the criteria for genetic testing in cases of severe early-onset obesity.
Learn to triage patients for specialist referral based on specific clinical signs.
key_facts: Endocrine testing is indicated when obesity is accompanied by short stature, falling height velocity, or delayed puberty.
Mildly elevated TSH with normal T3 and T4 often reflects adaptive changes in obesity, not primary hypothyroidism.
Testing for hypercortisolism is reserved for cushingoid features and growth deceleration, not simple weight gain.
Genetic testing is most useful in severe obesity starting before age 5 with hyperphagia, developmental delay, or dysmorphism.
Specialist referral and neuroimaging are prioritized for suspected hypothalamic or pituitary disease (e.g., rapid-onset obesity with headache or visual symptoms).
reference_quote: Endocrine testing is not routine in pediatric obesity; it is phenotype-driven. Broad 'endocrine panels' in typical primary obesity create false labels, increase cost, and delay structured obesity care. (Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline, 2017)
document_key_topics: Indications for endocrine testing in pediatric obesity
Common interpretation errors in thyroid function tests
Criteria for genetic testing in early-onset obesity
Specialist referral for hypothalamic or pituitary disease
